did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning classifiers, specifically Support Vector Machine (SVM) and 1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN), implemented in the Weka data mining tool, to classify dermoscopy images as normal or melanoma. This classification task is performed after feature extraction using Gabor Local Mesh Patterns and illumination correction algorithms.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including a novel illumination correction algorithm (Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning) and feature extraction method (Gabor Local Mesh Patterns). It details a specific methodology, uses a dataset of 200 dermoscopy images, and reports empirical results comparing classification accuracy and ROC curves using SVM and 1-NN classifiers. The title and content indicate it is an original technical study presented at the 2017 International Conference on Computational Science and Computational Intelligence.

title: Estimation of Illumination Map From Dermoscopy Images For Extracting Differential Structures Using Gabor Local Mesh Patterns
journal: 2017 International Conference on Computational Science and Computational Intelligence
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of uneven illumination in dermoscopy images to improve melanoma detection by proposing a novel illumination correction algorithm called Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning (WGWS). The authors combined this correction method with Gabor filtering and Local Mesh Patterns (GLMeP) for robust feature extraction from the Luminance channel of the CIELUV color space. The proposed approach was evaluated on a publicly available dataset of 200 dermoscopy images (160 nevi and 40 melanomas) acquired from Hospital Pedro Hispano. Classification performance was assessed using Support Vector Machine (SVM) and 1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN) classifiers via 10-fold cross-validation, measuring accuracy and ROC curves. The results demonstrated that the proposed WGWS-GLMeP method outperformed standard homogeneous texture features and homomorphic filtering, achieving the highest classification accuracy of 84.0% with SVM and an ROC of 0.70.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the dataset of 200 dermoscopy images (40 images, 20%). Classified as malignant/cancerous by an experienced dermatologist.
One of the two classes in the dataset of 200 dermoscopy images (160 images, 80%). Classified as normal by an experienced dermatologist.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopy images into 'normal' (nevus) or 'melanoma' (malignant/cancerous) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is to 'classify if a dermosopic image is normal or cancerous.' It uses Support Vector Machine (SVM) and 1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN) classifiers to predict discrete diagnostic labels (normal vs. melanoma) based on extracted texture features (WGWS-GLMeP). Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy and ROC curves.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermoscopy images acquired at Hospital Pedro Hispano, Matosinhos. The images were captured during clinical exams using a dermoscope at 20x magnification with a resolution of 765x573 pixels. The dataset was provided by the hospital for this research.

use.training: Used for classification experiments via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for classification experiments via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for classification experiments via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the SVM and 1-NN classifiers on the dataset of 200 dermoscopy images.
To compute an initial estimate of the illumination map using conditional distribution.
To estimate the illumination map by focusing on minimum Gaussian statistics of the image, avoiding complex conditional distribution computations.
Used in the Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning (WGWS) algorithm to compute error statistics and assign weights based on local standard deviation.
To ensure an unbiased selection criterion for pixels chosen in the vicinity of the central pixel during illumination correction.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification accuracy and ROC curves via 10-fold cross-validation, with comparative statements made based on these metrics without statistical significance testing.

Hospital Pedro Hispano Dataset.notes: Results were obtained using 10-fold cross-validation. The proposed WGWS-GLMeP method outperformed all other methods considered (HT, HF-HT, WGWS-HT). Classification algorithms showed worst performance when illumination correction was not used.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) and 1-Nearest Neighbor (1-NN) classifiers implemented in Weka data mining tool

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning (WGWS) for illumination correction followed by Gabor filtering and Local Mesh Patterns (GLMeP)
architecture_details.illumination_correction: Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning (WGWS) using Monte-Carlo posterior estimation and Gaussian statistics
architecture_details.texture_features: Gabor Local Mesh Patterns (GLMeP) extracted from the Luminance channel of CIELUV color space
architecture_details.lmeP_parameters: First three LMeP patterns (j=1, 2, 3) for a given (P, R), considering only uniform LMeP patterns

preprocessing_techniques: Color space conversion to CIELUV to decouple luminance (L) and chrominance (U & V) components
Logarithmic transform of the Luminance channel to separate illumination and reflectance components (additive model)
Illumination correction using Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning (WGWS) algorithm based on Monte-Carlo posterior estimation and Gaussian statistics
Comparison with standard homomorphic filtering (HF) for illumination correction
Manual segmentation of lesions by an experienced dermatologist to identify lesion regions
conclusions: The proposed Weighted Gaussian Window Scanning (WGWS) illumination correction algorithm, combined with Gabor Local Mesh Patterns (GLMeP) feature extraction, effectively addresses uneven illumination in dermoscopy images.
The proposed WGWS-GLMeP method outperformed standard homogeneous texture features, homomorphic filtering, and weighted Gaussian window scanning with homogeneous textures in melanoma detection.
The proposed method achieved the highest classification accuracy of 84.0% with an SVM classifier and an ROC of 0.70 on the Hospital Pedro Hispano dataset.
Illumination correction is critical for improving melanoma detection performance; classification algorithms showed the worst performance when illumination correction was not used.
Statistical estimation methods for illumination correction show good performance, and the proposed method based on local mesh patterns shows the best performance among the tested methods.
The image descriptor used in this study is not invariant to rotation and scale changes in the images, which is a limitation acknowledged by the authors.
The proposed technique extracts physiologically relevant differential structures, leading to better identification of melanoma in dermoscopy images.
important_remarks: The image descriptor (GLMeP) is not invariant to rotation and scale changes, a limitation explicitly acknowledged by the authors.
The study relies on a single, small dataset (200 images) from one institution (Hospital Pedro Hispano), raising concerns about generalizability and external validation.
The dataset is imbalanced, with 80% (160 images) being nevi and only 20% (40 images) being melanomas.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons were based solely on accuracy and ROC metrics.
Manual segmentation of lesions was performed by an experienced dermatologist, introducing potential subjectivity and lack of inter-rater reliability data.
The study is a conference paper (2017) with limited scope, focusing on preprocessing and feature extraction rather than deep learning or large-scale clinical trials.
Classification performance was moderate (84.0% accuracy with SVM), and the ROC curve for the best method was only 0.70, indicating limited discriminative power.
